Cats are naturally drawn to warm spots in winter, as their ideal ambient temperature is between 86 and 97 degrees Fahrenheit (30-36 degrees Celsius).
A cat's normal body temperature ranges from 100.5 to 102.5 degrees Fahrenheit, which is why they always seem to seek out the warmest place in the room.
Cats sleep more in winter, as shorter days and colder temperatures trigger longer rest periods, sometimes up to 20 hours a day.
Outdoor cats can develop hypothermia if exposed to temperatures below 32 degrees Fahrenheit (0 degrees Celsius) for extended periods.
Cats have specialized receptors in their paws that make them extremely sensitive to cold surfaces like snow or ice.
A cat's fur coat has two layers: a dense undercoat for insulation and longer guard hairs that repel moisture.
In winter, cats often sleep in a tight ball to minimize the surface area exposed to cold air and retain body heat.
Black cats absorb more solar heat on sunny winter days than lighter-colored cats due to the melanin in their fur.
Cats can get frostbite on their ear tips, tail, and paw pads during extreme cold, the same extremities at risk in humans.
Cats in colder climates naturally grow thicker winter coats, and indoor cats can do the same in response to subtle temperature cues.
Snow leopards, a wild relative of domestic cats, have the thickest, most insulating fur of any wild cat species.
Cats often press against radiators, heating vents, or fireplaces in winter, sometimes dangerously close, so always supervise them near open flames.
In winter, feral and outdoor cats sometimes hide inside car engines for warmth, so always knock on the hood before starting your car.
A cat's whiskers help them detect changes in air currents, including the cold drafts that come through windows and doors in winter.
Norwegian Forest Cats and Maine Coons evolved naturally in cold climates and have extra-thick, water-resistant double coats.
Cats can shiver just like humans when they get too cold, as it is the body's way of generating heat through muscle movement.
The Siberian cat breed is native to Russia and has a triple-layered coat designed to withstand the harshest winters.
In winter, outdoor water bowls for feral cats freeze quickly, and heated pet water dishes can be lifesavers for community cats.
Cats tend to eat slightly more in winter because their bodies need extra calories to maintain core temperature in cold environments.
Snow is fascinating to many cats, and their first encounter with it often results in cautious pawing and immediate retreat.
Indoor cats often stare out the window in winter, attracted by the movement of birds and squirrels seeking food in the snow.
Cats can sense pressure changes in the atmosphere, which is why they may seem more restless before a winter snowstorm.
Cold, dry winter air can cause static electricity to build up in a cat's fur, giving both the cat and their owner a small shock on contact.
Cats that live outdoors in winter require about 25% more calories to stay warm and healthy.
Paw-safe ice melt (pet-friendly salt alternatives) is important for households with cats, as traditional road salt can burn and poison their paws.
Some cats enjoy winter walks on harness and leash, provided they are gradually acclimatized and wear a pet-safe jacket.
The Turkish Van cat breed is sometimes called the 'swimming cat,' but even this water-loving breed avoids cold water in winter.
Cats' noses may feel cold and wet in winter, but a dry nose alone is not necessarily a sign of illness.
Long-haired cats like Persians and Ragdolls are naturally better insulated in winter than short-haired breeds.
A warm cat shelter for outdoor or feral cats can be made inexpensively from a Styrofoam cooler with a small entrance hole cut in the side.
Cats may experience a form of seasonal mood change in winter, becoming more lethargic as daylight hours shorten.
The Scottish Wildcat, one of the rarest wild cats on Earth, survives brutal Scottish winters in the Cairngorm Mountains.
Cats curl their tails around their bodies when resting in the cold to prevent heat loss, similar to arctic foxes.
Snow Bengals and other lighter-coated breeds can be harder to spot in snowy conditions, so always microchip your cat.
Antifreeze (ethylene glycol) smells sweet to cats and is extremely lethal, so clean up any spills immediately and keep it locked away.
In winter, cats appreciate having multiple cozy beds in warm areas of the home, as variety helps them find the ideal temperature spot.
A heated cat pad set to a low, safe temperature can provide older or arthritic cats with significant comfort during cold months.
Cats in the wild, like mountain lions, rely on large paw pads that act as natural snowshoes to navigate deep snow.
Indoor cats may develop dandruff in winter due to dry heating air, and adding a humidifier or omega-3 supplements can help.
Cats can suffer from seasonal joint stiffness in cold weather, especially older cats, and keeping them warm and gently active helps.
